entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 108
notes: The Sayings occupies ff. 198ra-199rb and lacks the first stanza found in other versions; the catalog explicitly corrects an older description of it as beginning imperfectly.
The Vision occupies ff. 199rb-200vb and is the sole witness of DIMEV 4814.

physicalDescription.dimensions: approximately 280 x 170-180 mm; written space approximately 230 x 140 mm
physicalDescription.extent: ii modern paper endleaves + i medieval parchment endleaf + 240 or 243 leaves; foliated 1-239; target poems on ff. 198ra-200vb
notes: Most of ff. 198r-201v is written in two columns; the relevant portion belongs to the 45-line layout used through f. 200v.
Both target poems are in Hand A. The catalog localizes that hand's scribal dialect to West Oxfordshire; this is not proof of either poem's composition place.
The catalog describes the codex as English, possibly Oxford, but marks Oxford as uncertain.

entities.description: Monda's article introduces and prints the previously unpublished Add. E. 6 roll witness. Its edited Middle English text occupies printed pp. 302-307; the preceding discussion describes the roll, dialect, date, provenance evidence, and relationship to Digby 86 and the other known versions.
entities.label: Joseph B. Monda, The Sayings of Saint Bernard from MS Bodleian Additional E 6 (1970)
notes: Monda expands abbreviations except the ampersand, supplies missing letters in italics, places conjectural readings for illegible text in italic brackets after comparison with other manuscripts, ignores manuscript punctuation he considers accidental, and follows the manuscript's capitalization.

Monda argues that Add. E. 6 is structurally closer to the prototype than Digby 86 because it omits a stanza he considers spurious, while also judging the Add. E. 6 copy metrically rough and textually corrupt. This is an attributed scholarly argument, not a settled stemma.

reviewNotes: Middle English is the language of both medieval poems. German occurs only in Horstmann's headings, byline, and editorial notes and is not transferred to the abstract works.
The Sayings and the Vision are independent work identities. They share Laud fol. 199r / IIIF canvas 413 because the first ends and the second begins on that manuscript surface.
Internet Archive's item metadata gives 1846 as a serial-level date, but the scanned volume title page and article supply 1874; 1874 is used for this volume and article.
Bernard of Clairvaux is a traditional association inherited through the poem and catalogs, not established authorship of the Middle English adaptation.

lineage.summary: CME00150 is the repository copy of a University of Michigan encoding of Carl Horstmann's complete six-page 1874 article, which prints two distinct anonymous Middle English poems from Bodleian Library MS. Laud Misc. 108: The Sayings of St. Bernard on ff. 198ra-199rb and The Vision of St. Paul on ff. 199rb-200vb. The two transmission paths share the edition, manuscript, and one boundary canvas but remain separately scoped. DIMEV 5215 identifies five Sayings witnesses: the Laud copy, Bodleian MSS Add. E. 6 (R) and Digby 86, British Library Harley MS 2253, and the Auchinleck Manuscript fragment. Complete public facsimiles of Laud, Digby, and the provider-published Auchinleck project are cached. Add. E. 6 has a catalog and mediated whole-roll reproduction route, plus Joseph B. Monda's 1970 scholarly edition in a cached complete journal-volume scan. Harley has the current catalog/request route, the complete METS scholarly edition, and Neil R. Ker's purchasable 1965 partial monochrome facsimile covering the target, but no verified public downloadable copy of Ker's volume. Abstract Middle English work nodes preserve the distinction between each witness and the underlying work, and qualified version-of paths connect the poems to the Pseudo-Bernardian Meditationes tradition and Latin Visio Pauli Redaction IV without inventing physical Latin exemplars. Brandes's 1885 printing of Redaction IV is a supporting edition, not the Vision translator's exemplar. Exact printed-page, folio, PDF-page, and IIIF-image mappings are recorded. Historical texts and editions are assessed separately from modern catalog data, scholarly editions, and manuscript photographs.

catalogingSubject.label: The Sayings of St. Bernard and The Vision of St. Paul
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Carl Horstmann's 1874 two-poem editorial selection as completely encoded in CME00150, not one medieval authored work. Part I is the anonymous Middle English Sayings of St. Bernard, a mortality and penitential poem loosely adapting Pseudo-Bernardian material and preserved in several versions. Part II is the anonymous Middle English Vision of St. Paul, a unique Laud version descending from the fourth redaction of the Latin Visio Pauli. Both are six-line stanzaic poems copied consecutively by Laud Misc. 108 Hand A, but they retain separate titles, incipits, DIMEV identities, source traditions, witness distributions, dates, and subjects. Horstmann's German title, byline, headings, and notes form a third editorial layer rather than part of either medieval work.

summaries.value: A two-poem editorial selection from Laud Misc. 108. The Sayings of St. Bernard urges self-knowledge and penance through reminders of bodily decay, conflict between flesh and soul, the three enemies of humankind, worldly vanity, unjust possessions, and death, ending with an appeal to Mary. The Vision of St. Paul follows Paul and Michael through punishments in hell, connects penalties to particular sins, recounts the removal of a soul to damnation and the reception of a good soul, and culminates in Christ granting the damned respite from Saturday noon until Monday morning before a closing prayer for grace at judgment. Horstmann prints both anonymous six-line stanzaic works directly from adjacent Laud leaves with German headings and textual notes.

Thirty-one six-line stanzas in the Laud version. The speaker invokes what Saint Bernard says in his book, meditates on death and bodily corruption, urges the soul to master the flesh and resist the World, Flesh, and Devil, condemns pride and unjust wealth, deploys an ubi sunt sequence, commends suffering and penitential discipline, and closes with prayer to Mary. Laud lacks the first stanza found in other versions but is not physically defective at the opening.

Forty-two six-line stanzas and 252 lines, uniquely witnessed in Laud Misc. 108. Michael guides Paul through a highly abbreviated vision of hell: hanging souls, a beast-filled river and narrow bridge, graduated depths of punishment, sealed pits, seven-colored fire, penalties for sexual, verbal, religious, and homicidal sins, and contrasting departures of a damned and a saved soul. Paul and Michael intercede, Christ grants the Sunday respite, and the poem closes by stressing hell's innumerable pains and praying to see Christ at judgment.

CME00150 must not be assigned one author, one DIMEV identifier, one source tradition, or one first-attestation date. The two poems are adjacent and formally coordinated in Laud and Horstmann's article, but DIMEV 5215 has a multi-witness Sayings tradition whereas DIMEV 4814 is a unique Laud Vision version; Pseudo-Bernardian Meditationes and Latin Visio Pauli redaction IV are likewise separate upstream work relationships. German editorial prose and the 1874 date remain edition-layer metadata.
